questions.scenario:
The incident channel has two saved lookups for the same hostname after an API cutover:

```text
$ dig @1.1.1.1 api.example.com A
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: 1010
;; ANSWER SECTION:
api.example.com. 60 IN A 93.184.216.99
;; SERVER: 1.1.1.1#53(1.1.1.1) (UDP)

$ dig @192.0.2.53 api.example.com A
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: 2020
;; ANSWER SECTION:
api.example.com. 2418 IN A 93.184.216.34
;; SERVER: 192.0.2.53#53(192.0.2.53) (UDP)
```

What is the most accurate resolver-to-answer summary?

options.text: `1.1.1.1` and `192.0.2.53` agree because both responses have status NOERROR
`192.0.2.53` is serving the new answer because its TTL is larger
Neither resolver answered because the output does not include `+short`

questions.explanation: The `SERVER` footer identifies which resolver answered, and the `ANSWER SECTION` shows the record it returned. Both responses are syntactically successful, but they disagree on the IP address.

questions.scenario:
A stale internal resolver returns this answer during a blue-green cutover:

```text
;; ANSWER SECTION:
api.example.com. 2418 IN A 93.184.216.34
;; WHEN: Wed Apr 15 14:32:00 UTC 2026
```

The authoritative record was changed to `93.184.216.99` ten minutes ago. What does the TTL evidence imply?

options.text: The resolver can keep returning the old cached address until the remaining TTL counts down
The resolver must query the authoritative server on every request because the TTL is nonzero
The authoritative server has deleted the record because stale answers always become NXDOMAIN
The browser should ignore DNS because TTL only applies to HTTP cache headers

questions.explanation: TTL is the DNS cache lifetime. A resolver that cached the old address can serve it until the TTL expires, which is why different clients can see different IPs during propagation.

questions.scenario:
A release engineer checks propagation with several public resolvers:

```text
Resolver 8.8.8.8:
93.184.216.99
Resolver 1.1.1.1:
93.184.216.99
Resolver 208.67.222.222:
93.184.216.34
Resolver 9.9.9.9:
93.184.216.99
```

What should the release note say?

options.text: Propagation is still in progress because one resolver still returns the old IP while the others return the new IP
DNS is complete because a majority of resolvers return the new IP
The application is down because public resolvers should never disagree
The record is missing because one resolver did not return a CNAME

questions.explanation: The article recommends checking multiple resolvers during migrations. If any resolver still returns the old address, some clients may still hit the old deployment until that resolver cache expires.

questions.scenario:
A support engineer reports that `dig +short api.example.com` from a laptop returns `93.184.216.34`, but `dig @1.1.1.1 +short api.example.com` returns `93.184.216.99`. The laptop's full output includes:

```text
;; SERVER: 127.0.0.53#53(127.0.0.53) (UDP)
```

What is the next best comparison?

options.text: Compare the default stub result with one or more explicitly selected resolvers to identify which resolver path is stale
Stop checking DNS and restart the web server because `+short` hides HTTP status codes
Assume the authoritative zone is broken because the laptop uses a loopback stub address
Delete the `A` record because two different answers prove the name is invalid

questions.explanation: The default lookup is answered by the configured resolver path, shown by the `SERVER` footer. Querying explicit resolvers with `@` is the right way to isolate which path still has a cached or different answer.

questions.scenario:
You need to post a concise DNS evidence summary in an incident channel. The saved output includes:

```text
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: 40719
;; ANSWER SECTION:
api.example.com. 60 IN A 93.184.216.99
;; Query time: 24 msec
;; SERVER: 1.1.1.1#53(1.1.1.1) (UDP)
;; WHEN: Wed Apr 15 14:30:01 UTC 2026
```

Which fields are essential for explaining resolver state during a migration?

options.text: The `SERVER` line, because it names the resolver that answered
The `ANSWER SECTION`, because it shows the record type and IP returned
The TTL value, because it tells how long this answer may stay cached
The terminal prompt username, because it proves which authoritative server owns the zone
The query status, because NOERROR versus NXDOMAIN changes the diagnosis

questions.explanation: Resolver identity, status, answer, and TTL are the evidence needed to reason about propagation and caching. A shell prompt or local username does not prove anything about authoritative ownership.

questions.scenario: A deployment checklist says the DNS TTL was lowered to 60 seconds five minutes before the IP change. Several customers still see the old IP with a TTL near 3500 seconds. What planning mistake most likely caused this?

options.text: The TTL was lowered too late, so some resolvers had already cached the old record using the previous longer TTL
The TTL should have been set to zero because DNS records cannot be changed while cached
The `A` record should have been replaced by an MX record before the migration
The resolver footer should have been removed so clients would trust the new answer

questions.explanation: The article explains that the old TTL is what existing resolver caches already honor. Lowering the TTL immediately before a change does not shorten cache entries that were created earlier under the old value.

questions.scenario: A teammate wants to use `dig +trace api.example.com` because the ordinary lookup returns SERVFAIL through one resolver. Which question is `+trace` best suited to answer?

options.text: Whether the delegation path from root to TLD to authoritative nameserver can lead to a final answer
Whether the browser accepted the site's TLS certificate after connecting
Whether the web server process is bound to TCP port 443
Whether the HTTP response contains the correct `Cache-Control` header

questions.explanation: The article describes `+trace` as replaying the full resolution path from root servers down. It is useful for delegation problems, not for TLS, process listeners, or HTTP response headers.
